14.2.1 Environment
We represent the environment by a 2D toroidal (wrap-around) grid. This choice
reduces the complexity of the simulation significantly, while maintaining enough
degreesoffreedomfortheartificialorganisms[3].Theenvironmentcontainsdiffer-
entsubstratesthatspreadoutwithinthegrid,minimizingthevariationofsubstrate
quantities between two neighboring vertices. Substrates diffuse at various speeds
andcaninteractwitheachotherthroughsimplifiedchemicalreactions,whichcre-
atenewsubstratesreleasingorconsumingenergy.Forexample,thetransformation
A+2B → C(+50)produces1unitofsubstrateC and50unitsofenergyfrom1
unitof Aand2unitsofB.Forsimplicity,theenvironmentstoresalistofallpossible
substratetransformations,andonlycellscantriggerthem.
14.2.2 Cells
Inthisenvironment,eachcellcontainsdifferentsensorsandhasdifferentabilities,
or “actions”. An action selection system allows a cell to choose the best action to
performduringthesimulation.Anactionoptimizationnetworkalsoallowscellsto
specialize,divisionafterdivision.Figure14.1givesaviewofourartificialcell.
Cells possess different density sensors located at each corner. The sensors are
specializedforeachdifferenttypeofsubstratesprovidedbytheenvironment.They
allow cells to measure the amounts of the corresponding substrates in their four
nearestneighbors.Thelistofsensorsandtheirpositionsinthecellisrepresentedin
thegenome.
Cellscanperformdifferentactions:transform(metabolize)substrates,absorbor
releasesubstratesintheenvironment,divide,wait,die,andsoon.Theimplementation
ofthemodelenablesasimpleadditionofactions.Aswithsensors,thegenomecan
activateorinhibitcellactions.Performinganactioncostsorbringsenergytothecells.

14.2.2.1 ActionSelectionSystem
Inordertoselectthebestactiontotrigger,cellspossessan“actionselectionsystem”,
presented in Fig.14.2. It consists of a set of rules by which cells use data arriving
throughthesensorstoselectthebestaction.Eachruleiscomposedofthreeparts:

Metabolismisoneofthemainfeatureofourmodel.Withouttheenergyprovided

organism’sfunctionanditsmetabolism.Theactionselectionsystemmustalsofind
awaytoextractenergyfromtheartificialchemistryprovidedbytheenvironment.

modelsintheliterature.Towardthisgoal,acentralfeatureofthesemodelsinvolves

14.2.4 Discussion
Thechemicallayerallowsthedevelopmentofsimpleorganismscomposedofaset
ofindependentcellsandorgans.Theorganismshavetofindametabolismadapted
to their environment before they can solve a given user-defined problem, and this
metabolismismadepossiblebytheuseofasimplifiedchemicalsimulator.
Grown organisms can generate their own particular shapes and functions, such
assubstrateprocessing.Inthislastcase,itisinterestingtoobservethedevelopment
of a morphology especially adapted to perform the requested function. Moreover,
organismsarecapableofself-repair:theycanbothregeneratetheirmorphologyand
recovertheirfunctionincaseofdamagetotheirstructure.Finally,wehaveshown
the capacity of multiple organs to cooperate toward producing complex structures
thatcantransportsubstrates.
Twocriticismscanbemadeatthispoint,however.First,generatedfunctionsare
simplistic:theymostlyinvolvesubstratetransportacrosstheenvironmentand,inthe
caseoftheself-feedingorganism,thepossibilitytotransformthesesubstratesduring

366 S.Cussat-Blancetal.
their travel. Obviously, this type of low-level behavior is not sufficient to generate
complex virtual creatures. The second shortcoming concerns shape generation. In
the above experiments, morphogens were intentionally placed by the user in the
environmentbeforetheorganismdeveloped.Thisisnaturallyanimportantlimitation
of the chemical layer, which must be later improved to obtain truly self-organized
morphogenesis.
14.3 HydrodynamicLayer
This section describes the interactions of an organism (grown as above) with the
substratesonthehydrodynamiclevelofourmodel.Itsmaincontributionisamethod
topositiontheembryonicmorphogensintheenvironment,takinginspirationfrom
the early gastrulation stage of real-world vertebrates [10]—a critical phenomenon
enablingorgandevelopmentinlaterstages.Byusingahydrodynamicsimulatorin
ourmodel,wewanttoseetheapparitionofsubstrateflowsintheenvironment,which
arecreatedbytheorganismwhenitperformsactionsinthechemicallayer,suchas
division,substrateabsorptionandrelease.Forexample,cellsmustbeabletoejecta
substrateinacertaindirectionwithacertainstrengthtoplaceitintheenvironment.
14.3.1 Implementation
Becauseofitsinherentcomplexity,thehydrodynamicsimulatoriscomputationally
costly. Therefore, we decided to use a method that reduced resource usage while
maintainingenoughrealismanddegreesoffreedom.Tothisaim,JosStam’snumer-
icalsolver[17, 18]isagoodcandidate:althoughmainlyusedforimageprocessing
needs,itsgreatadvantageistosimulatetheNavier-Stokesequations.Theintegration
of the hydrodynamic engine into our cellular model israther straightforward, as it
completelyreplacesthediffusionalgorithmpreviouslyusedtospreadsubstrates.In
Stam’smodel,theenvironmentisalsorepresentedbyagrid,inwhichtheparticlesof
fluidthatcontainsubstratesaremovingalongthevelocityvectorscalculatedbythe
solver.Inourenvironment,cellsconstituteobstacles:whenafluidparticlehitsacell
membrane,thevelocityvectoratthecollisionpointismodifiedsothatitredirects
theparticlealongthecelledge.Inafirststage,tosimplifythesimulation,different
substratesspreadindependently.
OneofthemainlimitationsofStam’smodel,however,isthenonconservationof
material quantity due to the simplification of the Navier-Stokes equations. During
thesimulation,thehydrodynamicenginecancauseasmalllossofmaterial,anerror
that is not compatible with our developmental system. The main purpose of the
hydrodynamic engine being to spread morphogens in the environment to support
the development of specific shapes, the slightest loss of material could generate
undesired creature growth. However, two methods exist to fix this problem. One
involvestheadditionofenergyconservationlawstocompensateforsubstrateloss.
Theotherappliesaproportionaldistributionofwastedmaterialontheentiregrid.We

14 ASynthesisoftheCell2OrganDevelopmentalModel 367
preferredthelatterbecausetheenergyconservationmethodisalsocomputationally
expensiveanddifficulttoapplytooursimulator.
To refine boundary conditions, we also subdivided the hydrodynamic grid with
respect to the chemical grid. Obviously, the smaller the grid resolution, the more
precisethecomputationofboundaryconditionsandfluidflows.Ontheotherhand,
sincegridsubdivisionsgreatlyincreasecomputationalneeds,weonlyappliedafactor
2inthiscase.Wealsoadaptedthealgorithmtotakeintoconsiderationinter-cellular
spreadingallowedbyourchemicalalgorithm.Becauseobstaclesrepresentedbycells
arestuckagainsteachother,nofluidflowispossiblebetweencells.Inourmodel,
however, the organism’s external velocity vectors are able to modify its internal
velocityvectorsinordertocreateinternalflows.
Finally,cellsinteractwiththeenvironment,particularlybyabsorbingorreleasing
substrates.Withoutahydrodynamiclayer,theiractionswouldnotcreatemolecular
movementsandfluidflows.Thankstothehydrodynamicengine,wecannowsimulate
thistypeofphenomenonbyassigningan“ejectionstrength”withaparticulardirection
toeachcell,whichallowsittopreciselypositionasubstrateintheenvironment.Cellular
flowsproduceglobalmotionintheenvironment.Inasimilarway,substrateabsorption
creates“suction”flows,whilecelldivisionsmodifytheenvironmentbygenerating
complexflows(especiallyvortices)whilearrangingemptyspotsforfuturecells.

14.4 PhysicalLayer
After developing creatures in the chemical and hydrodynamic layers, we wish to
extendourmodelbyaddingaphysicallayerbasedonaphysicsengine.Itspurposeis
toadddegreesoffreedomtothegrownorganisms.Weconductedvariousexperiments
toshowthecapabilitiesofsuchanextension.Thissectionpresentsthephysicsengine,
withtheexampleofamusclejointdevelopedinthechemicalandphysicallayersat
thesametime.
14.4.1 Implementation oftheLayer
Many physical simulators exist, each with its strengths and weaknesses. Adrian
BoeingandThomasBraunlpresentin[1]ausefulcomparativereviewofdifferent
free-of-use physics engines. They study various aspects such as realism, precision

14 ASynthesisoftheCell2OrganDevelopmentalModel 371
andefficiencyofthemainalgorithms(collisiondetection,links,materialproperties,
etc.).Integratingaphysicsengineintoourdevelopmentmodel,however,comeswith
manychallenges.Oneofthemostdifficultaspectisthelargenumberofobjectsthat
needtobemanagedinthephysicalworld,comprisingallthecells.Foreachobject,
collisionforcesandjointconstraintshavetobecomputedinatimethatmustremain
reasonabletoallowparallelsimulationwiththechemicalworld.Amongthereview
mentionedabove,wehavedecidedtochoosetheBullet physicsengine,seemingly
themostadaptedtoourneedsbecauseofitsefficiencyandrealism.
Theintegrationofthephysicallayerintothechemicallayerisrelativelysimple.
Eachtimeacellisborninthechemicallayer(whichcontrolscelldivision),anew
cubic object representing that cell is added to the physics engine. The new cell is
thenlinkedtoallitsneighboringcellsthroughfixedjoints.Whenacelldiesinthe
chemical layer, its corresponding object is also removed from the physics engine.
If physical constraints between two cells are too high in the physics engine (e.g.,
tension between cells), the cells are removed from the chemical layer, and so are
theircorrespondingobjectsfromthephysicallayer.

ities of association with the chemical layer. The same type of genome presented
in Sect.14.2.3.2 is used to develop an organism with a particular morphology
(Fig.14.15). In this case, it possesses a “kneecap” in the center (one cell), two
“bones” symmetrically disposed around the kneecap (about 400 cells each) and
various“musclefibers”thatlinkthetwobones(about100cellstotal).Inafirststage,

14.4.3 Discussion
Thephysicallayeraddsinterestingfunctionalitiestothemodel.Itsimulatesorgan-
ismsatanewscale,makingtheminteractwithavirtualworldconstitutedofother
artificialcreatures.Yet,itisalsodifficulttoconfigure,asitrequiresdefininginterac-

isthatcellspecializationintodifferentcelltypesiscurrentlyprogrammedbyhand,

14.5 AnL-SystemModelofMorphogenesis
Wepresentinthislastsectionanotherworkconceivedasanalternativetothechem-
icallayerofCell2OrgandescribedinSect.14.2.Thisnewmodelofmorphogenesis
doesnotinvolvecomputationallycostlyconcentrationsofchemicalsubstratesinthe
environment.Instead,itisbasedonL-systems,whichareaclassofgenerativemeth-
ods (named after Lindenmayer) using formal grammars of rewriting rules. In our
approach, molecular diffusion is replaced by “stem cells” associated with “attrac-
tion vectors”, generated by the L-system’s grammar. Through local propagation,
attractionvectorscanorientcellproliferationinvariouswayandpotentiallyproduce
anyshape.Weshowhereapossibleimplementationofthisconcept,derivedfroma
simplifiedversionofthepreviousdevelopmentalmodel.Thepotentialofthistype
ofapproachcanbeillustratedindependentlyfromthechemical,hydrodynamicand
physicallayersofCell2Organ.

14 ASynthesisoftheCell2OrganDevelopmentalModel 373
Inthismodel,thegeneticinformationofthecellsresidesintheL-systemandaset
ofparameters.Thisinformationimpactsthedirectionandprobabilityofdivisionof
certaincells.Thedirectionofdivisionispropagatedamongcellsbyproximityand
hereditysothatthemorphologycodedbytheL-systemisexpressedbythetendency
ofthecellstodivideinthesamedirection.
14.5.1 PrinciplesoftheModel
14.5.1.1 Cells
IntheL-systemmodel,cellscanbeoftwotypes:normalcells,whichconstitutethe
majorpartoftheorganism,andafewstemcells,whicharelaidoutinsuchaway
thattheyformthe“skeleton”ofthemorphology,asdescribedin[11].Normalcells
arecharacterizedbythreeparameters:
• anage,whichrepresentsthelifespanofthecellsinceitscreation
• adivisioncounter,whichstoresthenumberofdivisionsofthecell
• anattractionvector V ,whichrepresentsthepreferreddirectionofdivisionand
a
itsprobability(viaitsnorm)
Whenacelldivides,itgeneratestwocells:acopyoftheinitialcell,whosedivision
counterisincrementedby1,andanewcellwhosedivisioncounterissetto0.We
callthesetwocellsrespectivelymothercell anddaughtercell,i.e.,the“mother”is
theonethatcopiestheinitialparametersandhasahigherdivisioncounter.Thesame
ruleappliestostemcells.
Stem cells exploit the genetic information at the time of cellular division so as
to direct the growth of the organism. They are characterized by the same three
parametersasnormalcells,withtwoadditionalfeatures:
• anIDnumber,whichrepresentsarelationshipbetweenthecellandasegmentof
thegeneticinformation

firstcell)
A stem cell can perform two different types of division: an asymmetric division,
which produces a mother stem cell and a normal daughter cell, and a symmetric
division,whichproducestwostemcells.

14.6 ConclusionandFutureWork
Inthischapter,wehavepresentedanoverviewofourcompletedevelopmentalsys-
temCell2Organbasedonasimplificationofnaturalgrowthprocesses.Whereasmost
currentmodelsdonottakeintoaccountthemetabolicactivityofartificialorganisms,
Cell2Organsimulatesmetabolismbymeansofanartificialchemistryenvironment.
In addition, a new approach to morphogenesis based on L-systems was proposed.
TheL-systemweemploygenerates“attractionvectors”thatguidecellsduringdivi-
sion.This approach proved efficient togenerate complex shapes.Adifferentiation
mechanism was also included, whereby each terminal production of the L-system
assignedspecificcolorstothecells.Theresultwasasegmentationoftheorganism
intoregionscomparabletotheoneobtainedin[8].
We have also designed additional simulation layers to improve our organism’s
functionalities.First,ahydrodynamicsimulatorcomputesfluidflowsintheenviron-
ment,whichprovidesasolutiontotheproblemofmorphogenpositioning.Aninitial
organismplacesmorphogensintheenvironmentbyejectingsubstrateswithspecific
strengths, then other cells specialized in shape generation divide by following the
resultingpositionalinformation.Second,aphysicsenginesimulatesNewtonianlaws
inordertoproducehigher-levelbehaviorsuchasglobalorganismmovementsina
3D environment. Physical features bring our model closer to the ultimate goal of
developingcreaturesfromasinglecellwhichcanbehaveinthesamefashionasKarl
Sims’blockrobots[16].
To produce more complex creatures, we imagined a cell differentiation process
inspiredbybiology.Inreal-worldlivingsystems,afterundergoingacertainnumber
of divisions, embryonic stem cells generate new cell types, such as neurons, skin
cells, and so on. This mechanism can be exploited in our model to produce rota-
tionmovementsorothermorphologicaltransformationsincreatures.Forexample,
a“pusher”cellproducesaninitialmorphogeneticpattern.Cellsgrowingaroundit
possessagiven“divisioncredit”toproduceashape.Whenitscreditisconsumed,a
cellturnsintoapushercellthatproducesanewmorphogeneticpattern.Surrounding
developing cells continue shape growth following the previously produced pattern
and so on. A grammar based on L-systems could generate the division credit and

SaraMontagnaandMirkoViroli
Abstract Thehierarchicalorganisationofbiologicalsystemsplaysacrucialrolein
processesofpatternformationregulatedbygeneexpression,andinmorphogenesis
ingeneral.Inspiredbythedevelopmentoflivingorganisms,theabilitytoreproduce
a system’s dynamic at different levels of its hierarchy might also prove useful in
thedesignofengineeredproductsthatmanifestspatialself-organisingproperties.In
thischapter,wedescribeacomputationalframeworkcapableofsupporting,through
modelling and simulation, both the study of biological systems and the design of
artificial systems that can autonomously develop a spatial structure by exploiting
thepotentialofmultileveldynamics.Withinthisframework,weproposeamodelof
themorphogenesisofDrosophilamelanogaster reproducingtheexpressionpattern
in the embryo, then we examine a scenario of pervasive computing as a possible
applicationofthismodelintherealisationofengineeredproducts.
15.1 Introduction
Complexsystemsgenerallyexhibitahierarchicalorganisationthatplaysacrucialrole
intheirstaticanddynamicproperties.Thesepropertiesarehighlydependentuponthe
principlesofdownwardandupwardcausation,bywhichthebehaviourofthewholeis
conditionedbythebehaviouroftheparts(bottom-upemergence),whilethebehaviour
of the parts is also influenced, in turn, by the behaviour of the whole (top-down
immergence) [27]. An example is given by biological systems: they are organised
intodifferentlevels(proteins,cells,tissues,andsoon),whilethecontinuousinterplay
amongtheselevelsgivesrisetotheirobservedbehaviourandstructure.Inthiscontext,

This chapter is organised as follows: Sect. 15.2 starts by presenting the role of
hierarchicalorganisationinmorphogenesisandmotivatingtheneedforamultilevel

15.2 TheRoleofHierarchicalOrganisationinMorphogenesis
The hierarchical organisation of a complex system divides it into levels com-
posedofmanyinteractingelements,whosebehaviourisnotrigidbutself-organises
according to a continuous feedback between levels. In particular, an outstanding
property of all living systems is the tendency to form multilevel structures of

15 AComputationalFrameworkforMultilevelMorphologies 385
systems-within-systems.Eachlevelformsawholewithrespecttoitsparts,whileat
thesametimeitisapartofalargerwhole.Inbiologicalsystems,thefollowinghier-
archicallevelscanbeidentified:(1)sequences,(2)molecules,(3)pathways(suchas
metabolicorsignallingpathways),(4)networks(collectionsofmutuallyinteracting
pathways),(5)cells,(6)tissues,and(7)organs.Theinterplaybetweenlevelsranges
fromeventshappeningveryslowlyonaglobalscaleallthewaydowntothemost
rapideventsobservedonamicroscopicscale.Auniquemolecularevent,suchasa
mutation occurring under fortuitous circumstances, can be amplified to the extent
thatitchangesthecourseofevolution.Inaddition,allprocessesatthelowerlevels
ofthishierarchyareconstrainedby,andmustactinconformitywith,thelawsatthe
higherlevels.
Developmental biology is a fascinating branch of life science that studies the
processbywhichmulticellularorganismsgrow,focusingonthegeneticcontrolofcell
division,differentiationandmovement.Oneofitsmainchallengesisunderstanding
the mechanisms that make the process of regionalistic in a vertebrate embryo so
robust,ensuringthatanorganismisabletodevelopfromasinglecell(thezygote)
andacquirethesamemorphologyeverytime.
Animal life begins with the fertilisation of one egg. During development this
singlecellundergoes mitoticdivisionandcellulardifferentiationtoproducemany
differentcells.Eachcellofagivenorganismnormallyownsanidenticalcopyofthe
genome,thereforeitsdifferentiationisnotduetodifferentgeneticinformation,but
todifferentstatesofexpressionofthesamegenes.Thesubsetofgenesexpressedin
acellcontrolscellproliferation,specialisation,interactionsandmovement,henceit
correspondstoaspecificcellbehaviourandroleintheentireembryo.
Onepossiblewaytocreatecelldiversityduringembryogenesisistoexposecells
todifferentenvironmentalconditions.Extracellularstimuliarenormallygenerated
bysignalscomingfromothercells,eitherbydirectcell-to-cellcontact,ormediated
bycuesthattravelthroughtheenvironment.Inthefirstcase,onlyadjacentcellsare
involved.Themostcommonphenomenonislateralinhibition,whereinitiallysimilar
cellsstartacompetitionthatdrivesthemtobecomedifferentfromoneanother:one
celloragroupofcellsemergesasthe“winner”,specialisingintoonecelltypeand
inhibitingtheneighbouringcellsfromdoinglikewise.Lateralinhibitionistherefore
oneprocessbywhichprecisepatternsofdistinctcelltypescanbegenerated[2,14].
Anotherstrategyfordrivingcellstowardaspecificfateisinductiveinteraction,where
cellsarepushedintoadevelopmentallineagethroughsignallingmoleculesthatcan
behaveeitherasshort-range(directcontact)orlong-rangeinducers(diffusion),also
calledmorphogens.
On the side of intracellular dynamics, signalling pathways and gene regulatory
networks are the means by which cell diversity is achieved. Signalling pathways
convertanexternalsignalintoinformationthatcantravelinsidethecelland,inmost
cases, affect the expression of one or more target genes. They are activated as a
consequenceofbindingbetween(i)acueintheenvironmentandareceptorinthe
cell’smembraneor(ii)twomembraneproteinsbelongingtoadjacentcells.Binding
causestheactivationofdownstreamproteinsuntilatranscriptionfactor,thatactivates
orinhibitstheexpressionoftargetgenes,isproduced.Duringembryogenesis,fewof

thesepathwaysareactiveandworkmostlyasmutualinhibitorsormutualenhancers.
Themainprincipleisthatmutualenhancersareactiveinsideasameregionandtheir
interactiongivesrisetopositivefeedback,whilepathwaysactiveindifferentregions
generallyworkasmutualinhibitors.Insomeboundaryregions,wecanalsoobserve
agradientofactivityofthedifferentgroupsofpathways,duetotheinhibitoryeffect

15.3 ComputationalFramework
Inthissectionwefirstdescribethecomputationalmodelthatconstitutesthefounda-
tionofourwork,thentheMS-BioNET(MultiScale-BiochemicalNETwork)surface
language,andfinallythemainfeaturesofthesimulationengine.1
The calculation that we rely upon is rather simple, yet it also allows to model
complex simulation scenarios. These can involve large networks of compartments
(biologicalsystemscaneasilycontainseveralthousandsofthem),withspecifictopo-
logicalstructures(e.g.,lattice,torus,scale-freenetwork),andspecificcompartment
characteristics (e.g., chemical laws, initial concentrations of chemical substances,
outgoinglinkrates).Therefore,asfarasthepracticalaspectofthesimulationmethod-
ologyisconcerned,itisratherimportanttodesignasurfacelanguagethatcan,onthe
onehand,providesuitableconstructstoeasilyexpresstheabovecases,andonthe
otherhand,intuitively“compile”toacomputationalmodel.Finallyanefficientsim-
ulationengineabletoexecutesuchacomputationalmodelisnecessarytoperform
simulationsinreasonabletime.
15.3.1 TheComputational Model
Theproposedcomputationalmodelofmulticompartmentbiochemicalnetworksisa
directextensionofGillespie’sbasicchemicalmodel[13],whichrealisesadiscrete
andstochasticsimulationofasystem.IntheGillespiemodel,achemicalsystemis
viewedasauniquewell-mixedsolution.Everymoleculeisexplicitlymodelledand
everyreactioninwhichtheycantakepartisexplicitlysimulatedonthebasisofthe
stochastic simulation algorithm (SSA). Once the system has been initialised, i.e.,
molecules,reactionsandreactionrateshavebeendefined,thesimulationproceeds
by choosing the next reaction to occur on the basis of a random number and its
propensityfunction,calculated fromthereactionrateandthenumberofreactants.
The time interval to update the simulation time is also computed step by step as a
functionofarandomnumberandthesumofpropensityfunctionsofallreactions.
Theiterationofthesestepsconstitutesthesimulation.

As such, each link of the graph is characterised by a rate dictating the speed of

This computational model is able to integrate what happens inside a compartment
with what happens outside, thus qualifies as a multilevel model. For instance, we
areabletoreproducetheintracellulargeneticnetworksandsignallingpathwaysofa
multicellularorganismintheformofreactionsinsidethecompartments,while,atthe
sametime,wecanalsocapturethecell-to-cellcommunicationphenomenahappening
onelevelhigherthroughtheexchangeofmoleculesacrosscompartments.Toremain
within the scope of this chapter, however, we do not provide further details of our
modelherebutinvitetheinterestedreadertoreferto[18].

The back-end engine is a C++ program that receives the intermediate file and
accordinglyinitialisesalltheproperdatastructures.Thesimulationprocessisbased
on the optimised Gillespie’s algorithm, called Next Reaction Method, described in
[11] and used in [28]. This algorithm is particularly efficient because it takes a
computationaltimeproportionaltothelogarithmofthenumberofreactions,instead
of the number of reactions itself. It mainly consists in the computation, step by
step,ofonlyasmallportionofthereaction’spropensityfunctionsusingproperdata
structures that store the dependency between propensity functions and reactions.
Moreover it suggests the reuse, under certain conditions, of the time interval to
updatethesimulationtimepreviouslycomputed.
Thesimulatorhasbeenmodifiedandadaptedtohandleourcomputationalmodel
inthefollowingmanner:

transition in the system, i.e., one chemical reaction inside each compartment of
thenetwork,andadditionally,alltheactionsinvolvingtransfersthroughlinks.
• A dependency graph is built that links each actiona to those whose rate should
changewhenaisexecuted.Thisextendsthebasicdefinitionof[11]withtheidea
thatalinkactionpossiblyalsoaffectschemicalreactionsinthetargetcompartment.
Theengineproducesanoutputtextfileaccordingtooutdeclarationsinthespecifi-
cation,whichcanbeusedtoproducechartsusingstandardtoolslikespreadsheets,

15.4.1 Biological Background
Afterfertilisation,thedistributionofmRNAmoleculesinsidetheeggofDrosophila
isnotuniformandappearsalreadypolarised.ThesemRNAsarematernallyprovided
and called maternal effects. During the first 72 min of the animal’s life, 9 nuclear
divisionsoccur;theyaresynchronousandfast.Duringthenext4divisions,thesetof
generatednucleimigratefromthemiddleoftheeggtowardsthesurface.Thisstage
ofanimaldevelopmentiscalledsyncytialblastoderm.Startingfromcleavagecycle
13,plasmamembranesgrowtoencloseeachnucleus,creatingtheso-calledcellular
blastoderm,whichconsistsofabout6,000cells.
Untilthecellularblastodermstage,maternaleffectsarethemaincauseofdevel-
opment.Aftercellularisation,thedivisionrategreatlyslowsdownwhilethezygotic
transcriptionofcellsnoticeablyincreases.
Once cellularisation is completed, the regionalisation by gene expression is

ontheanterior-posterioraxis,whosepatterningoriginatesfromtheinhomogeneous
distributionofmaternaleffectsintheegg,inparticularbicoid andhunchback (for
theanteriorparts),nanosandcaudal(fortheposteriorparts).Theyaretranscription
factorsthatdrivetheexpressionofgapgenessuchashunchback(hb),Krüppel(Kr),
knirps(kni)andgiant(gt),asshowninthediagramofFig.15.5[2,12].Tailless(tll)
alsoappearsasagapgene,butitsregulationisnotrepresentedhere,whilenanosis
notexplicitlymodelledasitsmaineffectistheregulationofcaudal.
Continuingwiththecascadeofregulation,thedownstreamtargetsofbothmater-

lishaprecisespatialconfiguration,i.e.,respectively7and14segments.Anexample
of regionalistic is shown in Fig. 15.3 for the pair-rule gene even-skipped (green)

15.5 APervasiveComputingApplicativeScenario
Inthiswork,wearguethatthebiologicallymotivatedtheoreticalframeworkdescribed
above in the case of biological modelling can also prove useful for the engineer-
ing of self-organising morphogenetic systems, i.e., artificial systems that organise
themselvesbyexploitingbio-inspiredarchitecturingprocesses.Theexperimentwith
DrosophilainSect.15.4illustratedthecriticalroleplayedbymoleculardiffusionand
reactionsinspatialpatterning,andwebelievethatthesameprinciplescanapplyto
ascenarioofpervasivecomputing,too.
The key biological phenomenon of morphogen diffusion in the environment,
which creates fields of substances reacting with other substances to generate pre-
cise spatial structures, is a fruitful metaphor for artificial systems design. There,
different“entities”,suchasservices,mayalso“diffuse”andautonomouslydistrib-
utethemselvesinthenetworktoreachdifferentareasoftheinfrastructure,wherethey
can“react”withotherentities,suchasrequests.Thisideaisextensivelyexploitedin
ourresearch[30],whichwesummarisehere.

15 AComputationalFrameworkforMultilevelMorphologies 397
15.5.1 PervasiveServiceSystemsandSpatialComputation
Today’snetworkscenariosfaceanincreasingavailabilityofpervasivesensingand
actuatingdevices(RFIDtags,PDAs,localisationdevices),whichcandenselypop-
ulateoureverydayenvironmentswithdigitalinformationaboutusersandlocations,
and tightly integrate with the Web—seen as a shared space for providing business
as well as social networking services. This will eventually lead to the emergence
ofverydense,spatiallydistributedinfrastructuresprovidinggeneral-purposedigital
services: traditional Web services (social networks, video broadcasting) enriched
with new means of interaction (e.g., smaller displays and cameras) and exploiting
contextual information; pervasive location-based information services (finding the
nearestpost-office,marketgoods,etc.);servicestocoordinateactivitiesofsituated
users (intelligent lights and signs, user-adaptive advertisement displays), services
thatencapsulatepervasivedevicesandmakethemaccessibleandretrievableinthe
network (e.g., by computational fields that facilitate retrieval in case of network
mobility[17]),andsoon.
In this context, any coordination infrastructure supporting such highly decen-
tralised and dynamic scenarios should act as a kind of “distributed space”, which
would reify the existence of services and support their interaction and evolution.
Propercoordinationlawsmustbedesignedanddeployedacrossthisdistributedspace
to guarantee properties of self-organisation, self-adaptation and self-management,
towardagreatertoleranceforopennessinservicefunctionality,userdataanduser
needs.
In this framework, the notion of pervasive service can be invoked to uniformly
represent concepts such as software functionality, devices, data, knowledge, and
signals.Likeamorphogen,apervasiveservicecanbeinjectedinonelocationand
diffusearound,interactingandcompetingwithotherservicesinacontext-dependent
manner inside each node of the network. Accordingly, each pervasive service will
happentoeffectivelyworkinaregionofthewholespace,composedofoneormore
“niches” where the context is favourable. Namely, the stateand behaviour of each
service is to be understood as a spatial concept, while the dynamics of the whole
systemofpervasiveservicesisnaturallyseenasaformofspatialcomputation.
Hencethepossibilitytoadoptinthisframeworkrulesinspiredbymorphogenetic
processes,whichcanbeusefultocontroltheemergenceofaproperspatialstructure,
suchasthenicheswhereservicescanautonomouslybedistributed.Suchservices,
formingasortof“ecology”asarguedin[32],shouldbemanagedbyaninfrastructure
addressingthefollowingissues:
• SpatialDistribution.Thenetworksoverwhichtheinfrastructureisdeployedcan
span different sizes (from large-scale sensor networks, such as a traffic control
system,tosmaller-scalenetworks,suchastheintranetofacompany,amarket,or
amuseum),topologies(fixedfortrafficsensors,ordynamicwhenPDAsenterthe
picture), and metrics (the actual distance in space, number of hops to reach one
another,communicatingbandwidthofnetworklinks,andsoon).Theinfrastructure

398 S.MontagnaandM.Viroli
shouldthengenerallyhandleapossiblydense“space”oflocalities(nodes)where
serviceslive(run,move,diffuse).
• Context-Awareness.Theinfrastructureshouldnaturallymatchtheinherentspa-
tiallyandsociallysituatednatureoftheservices,theirusers,andtheirenvironment.
Insomeregionsofthenetwork,servicesmightfindasuitablecontexttooperate,
whileinotherstheymightsimplybeunnecessaryandthusberemovedforthesake
ofoptimisation.Hence,theinfrastructureshouldbeabletoadaptitscoordination
behaviourtothecontextualinformationavailable,andtothelogicalandphysical
characteristicsofeachlocationanditsproximity.
• Adaptivity.Propertiesofself-organisation,self-adaptationandself-manage-ment
mustbeinherentlyenabled,includingthepossibilityofservicestoautomatically
bindtoeachother(composeormatchrequestandresponse),decaywhennotbeing
used,movetomorefavourableregionsofspace,competefortheuseofcommon
resources,adaptivelyretrieveeachother,andsoon.Sincethenetworkishighly
decentralised and highly dynamic, such behaviour shouldbe obtained without a
globalmanagermonitoringtheoverallnetwork,butratherbyemergencefromthe
localinteractionsofservicesineachnode.
• Openness. The infrastructure should be able to tolerate (even in the long-term)
openmodelsofserviceproductionandusage,addressingunforeseennewclassesof
servicesprovided,newuserneeds,andchangesinnetworktopology.Inprinciple,
anyrepairofsystemapplicationsshouldbeseenwithinthemodel,namely,through
properservicesinjectedinthenetworktoimproveorchangeon-the-flyexisting
ones,movingtheoverallsystembehaviourtowardsanewequilibrium.
Accordingly,wecanenvisiontheinfrastructureforpervasiveservicesasakindof
spatialcomputer,runninginapossiblylargeandmobilenetwork,andprovidinga
groundforspatiallydistributedservicestobeinjected,globallyorpartiallydiffuse,
interactandcompetewitheachother,spatiallyandtemporallydiminishanddecay,
andcontextualiseineachsubregionofthespace.
The problem of finding good metaphors for designing and implementing this
kind of infrastructure has been typically addressed in the literature by relying on
nature-inspiredapproaches:physicalmetaphors[9,16],chemicalmetaphors[5,31],
biologicalmetaphors[4, 6],togetherwithapproachesfocusingonhigher-leveleco-
logical/socialmetaphors (e.g.,trophicnetworks[1, 29]).Inthiswork,wepropose
morphogenesisandbiochemicalworkinglawsaspropermetaphorstosuitablysup-
port the required self-x features that pervasive computing calls for. In particular
thecomputationalmodelandoverallframeworkpresentedinSect.15.3seemtous
particularlypromisingforaddressingsuchscenarios.
In the present biochemical inspiration, pervasive services are associated with a
numericactivityor“relevance”valueresemblingachemicalconcentrationandmea-
suringtheextenttowhichservicescaninfluencethecoordinationstate.Forexample,
servicesatalowconcentrationwouldberatherinert,takingpartincoordinationonly
atalowfrequency.Byrelyingonproperchemical-likelaws,tobeestablishedinto
each location of the distributed infrastructure (i.e., each node and compartment of

15 AComputationalFrameworkforMultilevelMorphologies 399
thegraph),concentrationofserviceswillbeautomaticallychangedovertimesoas
tomakeinterestingself-xcoordinationpropertiesemerge.
15.5.2 SpatialCoordination Patterns
In this section we explain through two examples how appropriate chemical laws
can support interesting scenarios of spatial coordination using key morphogenetic
mechanisms,namely:fieldformationandgradientascent.
15.5.2.1 FieldFormation
Computational fields, also known as gradients [7],constitute a key building block
of pervasive computing systems [17]. They are distributed data structures that are
initiated(i.e.,“pumped”)fromasourcenodeanddiffuseintheirsurroundinguntil
eachnodeofthenetworkreachesastablefieldlevel,called fieldvalue,whichshould
onlydependontheestimateddistanceofthenodefromthesource.Thisspatialdata
structureisprimarilyused toprojectsome datafromthesource toaneighbouring
region,sothatall“agents”inthatregioncannotonlybeawareofthisdata,butalso
retrieve the source by simply moving on a step-by-step basis towards the source.
It means choosing at each step the neighbouring node whose field value indicates
that itis closer tothe source. This mechanism can be primarilyseen as physically
inspired,foritmimicsthewaygravitationalfieldswork[17],butitalsoresembles
diffusionmechanisms,whetheratthemolecularlevelorathigherbiologicallevels
suchasininsect“stigmergy”(wherebytracesleftintheenvironmentstimulatethe
behaviourofotheragents).Forinstance,inthepervasivedisplayinfrastructure,fields
can be pumped by displays with specific characteristics (e.g., a very wide screen
may be willing to attract suitable visualisation services), by user profiles (a user
interestedinsportsattractsservicesvisualisingsportnews),orevenbyvisualisation
services(attractingprofilesofusersinagivenproximity,soastoreasonaboutwhich
visualisationpolicyshouldbeusedinthenearfuture).
Inourframework,therecanbemanywaysofcreatingafieldthroughdiffusion
laws.Wefocushereononeexample,whoseprogramintheMS-BioNETlanguage
wasshowninTable15.2andisdescribedbythefollowingchemicalreactions:

node.The(PUMP)rulebeginsbyspawningunitsoftheFIELDservice,suchthat
theFIELDconcentrationstartsrising.Ontheotherhand,the(DECAY)rulemakes

15.6 RelatedWork
ThecomputationalmodelpresentedinSect.3.1isinspiredbyMembranecomputing
techniques[19]formallycalledPsystems,whichconsistofamembranecontaininga
multisetofobjects(representingchemicalsubstances)thatchangeovertimeaccord-
ing to given dynamical rules (representing reactions). Each membrane encloses a
compartmentthatcanitselfbeenclosedinanothercompartment,formingahierar-
chyofembeddedcompartments.In[24],thePsystemsformalismhasbeenextended
withstochasticsemanticsbyintegratingtheGillespie’sSSA.Inawayverysimilar
toourapproach,reactionstakingplaceinsideeachcompartmentaremodelledwith
the SSA, while the system’s topology is given by the compartment hierarchy and
interactionsbetweencompartmentsarederivedfromthePsystemrules.
TheMS-BioNETlanguagecanalsobecomparedtolanguagesbelongingtothe
researchfieldcalledspatialcomputing,mostnotablyProto[6]andMGS[25].Proto
is a language dedicated to programming the behaviour of very large distributed
systems,inwhichanynodeperformslocalcomputationsandinteractswithalimited
neighbourhood.ByusingProto,thedesignercanprogramatahigh-levelintermsof
functionalcomputationoverspatialcomputationalfields.Themaindifferenceswith
ourlanguagearethefollowing:
(i) differentiationisachievedinProtobyamechanismof“sensing”(byintercepting
agivensignal,adevicecanperformspmecustomcomputation),whileinour
frameworkwecanspecifydifferentbehavioursineachcell;
(ii) theProtolanguageoffersahigherlevelofabstraction,independentfromcon-
cernsaboutnetworktopology(thesearehandledattheleveloftheplatform),
while our language has explicit features to specify this topology in a flexible
manner;
(iii) theonlyformofinter-deviceinteractioninProtoisamechanismofautomatic,
asynchronousbroadcastingofmessagestoallneighbours,whileourtransferof
moleculesisunidirectionalandprobabilistic.
MGSisaprogramminglanguagethatsupportsthemodellingandsimulationofsys-
temswithacomplexstructurethatmightchangeovertime,i.e.,dynamictopologies.

15 AComputationalFrameworkforMultilevelMorphologies 403
In[24],MGSisusedtoimplementastochasticPsystem.Inouropinion,thesyntaxof
thelanguagemightbesomewhatdifficulttomasterforusers,suchasbiologists,who
maynothaveaformalbackgroundincomputerscienceorasufficientprogramming
experience.
The abilitytomodel biochemical networks isalsooffered by anumber of sim-
ulation tools specialised in biochemical kinetics (see a survey in [3]). These tools
address the problem mostly at the level of a graphical user interface (GUI), with
littlesupporttoflexiblyexpresslarge-scaleanddynamicnetworksatthelanguage
level. One advantage of process calculi is that they are designed to retain Turing
expressiveness,hencepotentiallyallowtoorganiseandstructureanycomplexsimu-
lationscenario.Practically,however,thiscanproveveryhardandgenerallyrequires
a higher-level language to be compiled into the process language. Moreover, the
majorityofkinetictoolsarebasedonmathematicalmodels(ODE,PDE)andfocus
mainly on a single intracellular network. A few of them (CellDesigner, CellWare,
COPASI,Dizzy,GEPASI,JDesigner,VirtualCell,MesoRD)alsosupportthedefin-
itionandrepresentationofintermediateintracellularcompartments,butitbecomes
difficulttomodellargenetworksofcompartmentspreciselybecausethespecification
oftheirbehaviourandstructureisnormallydonebyhandthroughaGUI.
Currently, our model only supports the representation of static networks. This
already allows to model interesting morphogenetic scenarios, but future work will
bededicatedtoaconsistentlyextendingitwithadynamichierarchyofmultiplenested
compartments,involvingaction-molecules(similartofiringmolecules),whichwill
causelinkrepositioning,compartmentcreation,splitting,joining,andsoon.
15.7 Conclusions
The process of spatial self-organisation was shown to be highly dependent on the
interplay between the dynamics at different levels of a biological system’s hierar-
chicalstructure.Whenmodellingandsimulatingphenomenaofmorphogenesis,or
designingartificialsystemsthatmanifestedpropertiesofspatialorganisationssuch
astheonesobservedinlivingsystems,itwasappropriatetoreproducesuchahier-
archy.Inthisworkwehavedescribedacomputationalframeworkandprogramming
language that we have developed to support such a multilevel dynamics. On both
sides, the simulation of real phenomena and the design of artificial systems, the
resultsshownwerepromisingandgavesupporttoourthesis.
Concerning artificial systems design, the morphogenetic metaphor, along with
biochemicallawsandthepossibilityofmodellingchemicaldiffusion,playsacrucial
role in enabling programmable models to address the requirements of engineering
adaptive pervasive services. In particular, we showed that with very simple bio-
chemicallawsitwaspossibletomodelareaction-diffusionbehaviourthatgenerated
usefulspatialpatterns.Indeed,ourmodelpromotesaviewofpervasivesystemsas
spatialcomputersandaviewofadaptiveservicesinthemasspatialstructuresthat
candiffuse,ascendfieldsandperformotheractions.Thisopensthewaytothestudy

404 S.MontagnaandM.Viroli
of other self-organisation and pattern formation processes, and their use in future
engineeringresearch.

Part IV
Generating

Chapter 16
Interaction-Based Modeling of Morphogenesis
in MGS
AntoineSpicher,OlivierMichelandJean-LouisGiavitto
Abstract Inthischapter,weadvocateadomainspecificlanguage(DSL)approach
to overcome the difficulties of modeling and simulating morphogenetic processes.
A careful discussion of the design goals of a DSL leads to the development of
an experimental programming language called MGS. Its declarative approach is
based on the notion of topological collection originating from algebraic topology.
Topologicalcollectionsarisenaturallywhenmodelinga“dynamicalsystemwitha
dynamic structure”, or (ds)2, as the state of the system. The evolution function of
thesystemisspecifiedbyatransformation,whichisasetofrewritingruleswhere
each rule defines a local interaction. We illustrate these notions through different
modelsofthesamemorphogeneticprocess:thegrowthofaT-shapedstructure.The
objectiveistoshowhowavarietyofmodelscanbeconsistentlyhandledwithinthe
MGSframework.
16.1 Introduction
Mostresearchworkspresentedinthisbookrelyonsimulationtomodel,exploreand
analyzethebehaviorofengineeredmorphogeneticprocesses.Computersimulation
is a tool of choice, if not the only systematically available one, for their design.

In this context, making the implementation of computer simulation faster, easier,
andreusableisabsolutelycrucial.Yet,themodelingandsimulationofsuchsystems
remaintodaydifficultanderror-proneforatleasttworeasons:
• The spatial organization of morphogenetic systems is dynamic and requires
advancedcomputerrepresentations,oftenatdifferentscales.
• Models of morphogenetic processes require a coupling between patterning and
development: the evolution of a system’s spatial structure depends on its state,
whileatthesametimetheevolutionofitsstatedependsonitsspatialstructure.
Thissituationoftenleadstothedevelopmentofadhocsimulationframeworks,
whicharededicatedtoaparticularproblemandrelyonaparticularspatialrepresen-
tation.Incontrast,thischapteradvocatesadomainspecificlanguage(DSL)approach
tocopewiththedifficultiesencounteredwhenelaboratingacomputersimulationof
naturalandengineeredmorphogenesis.
DesigningaDSLfortheSimulationofMorphogenesis
DSLsarespeciallytailoredprogramminglanguagesdesignedtosolveproblemsina
particulardomain[41].Tothisend,theyprovideusefulabstractionsandnotationsfor
thedomainathand.Theyaremoreattractivethangeneral-purposelanguagesforpro-
gramminginadedicateddomainduetotheireaseofcoding,systematicreuse,better
productivity,reliability,maintainability,andflexibility[61].Furthermore,DSLsare
usuallysmallanddeclarative1 asopposedtoimperative.
Comparedwithadhocsimulationplatforms,alanguagededicatedtothemodeling
andsimulationofmorphogenesisoffersmoreexpressivepowerandwiderapplica-
bility.Forinstance,itcanfacilitatethecomparisonofvariousmorphogeneticmodels
byprovidingauniformandconsistentenvironmentfortheirdescriptionandsimula-
tion.Itcanalsoleveragethe“know-how”gatheredfromvariousmodelsbyenabling
modelsharing,reuseandcoupling.
Yet,theDSLapproachmustalsofacedifficultiessimilartotheadhocapproach.
While it addresses the need for generality, there is no unifying formalism that can
includeallmorphogeneticprocesses.The“wholesale”modelingofmorphogenesis
wouldmobilizeagreatnumberofmodelsofmanytypes(genetic,mechanical,chem-
ical,etc.)requiringawiderangeofformalisms(fromcontinuoustodiscrete,from
deterministic to stochastic) and styles (for example, some models adopt a space-
centricview,whileothersrelyonanagent-centricview).
Shortofintegratingthisdiversityatatheoreticallevel,itisneverthelesspossibleto
developaframeworktounifyprogrammingforsimulationpurposes.Thefeasibility
ofcode-levelunificationisbasedonthreekeyfindings:
1Declarativeprogrammingfocusesonwhatshouldbecomputedinsteadofhowitmustbedone.
Objectsandconstructionsareclosetothemathematicalstandardsthatenableaneasiermathematical
reasoningonprograms.Thusadeclarativeprogramisanexecutablespecificationnotburdenedby
theimplementationdetailsandremainsclosetothemathematicalmodel.

16 Interaction-BasedModelingofMorphogenesisinMGS 411
• Despite the variety of formalisms and styles used in the simulation of morpho-
genesis,thevastmajorityofthemcapturemorphogeneticprocessesasdynamical
processes.However,thestructureofthesedynamicalsystemsisitselfdynamic.
• Space-centered or entity-centered modeling can be reconciled through an
interaction-oriented view. The problem is then to offer sufficiently expressive
meansofspecifyingsophisticatedinteractions.
• Thevariousspatialorganizationsthatunderliethestateofadevelopmentalsystem
can be subsumed under the abstract viewpoint of topological chains. However,
thisdoesnotpresumethedifficultiesofachievinganeffectiveimplementation.
Basedontheseobservations,theMGSprojectstartedintheearly2000swiththe
goalofelaboratingaDSLdedicatedtothemodelingandsimulationofmorphogen-
esis [22]. Since then, the notions investigated in MGS have been validated by the
simulationofnumerousexamplesofprocessesinvolvedinmorphogenesis,suchas
self-assembly[5,28,53],modelsofgeneregulatorynetworks[21],systemsbiology
(mainlyatacellularlevel)[25,42,50,54],syntheticbiology[19],plantgrowth[48]
andothernaturaldevelopmentalcases[30, 51].
OutlineoftheChapter
Intherestofthisintroductorysection,weelaborateupontheabovethreefindings,as
theyconstitutetherationalebehindMGS.However,thisdiscussionisnotaprerequi-
siteforunderstandingtheself-containedpresentationoftheMGSlanguageexposed
inSect.16.2.Thispresentationfocusesonthenotionsoftopologicalcollectionsand
transformationsusedtorepresentrespectivelythestateofamorphogeneticprocess
anditsevolutionfunction.Section16.3illustratestheseconstructionswithdifferent
modelsofthesameexample:thedevelopmentofaT-shapedstructure.Theobjective
is to show how a variety of models can be handled consistently within the MGS
framework.
16.1.1 (DS)2:CouplingPatterningandDevelopment
Inhisfamouslastpublicationfrom1952entitled“TheChemicalBasisofMorpho-
genesis”[60],AlanTuringelaboratesadynamicalsystemsviewofmorphogenesis,
wherehecharacterizesadevelopingorganismasasetofvariablesthatchangeover
timeandcapturethestateofthesystemalongwithitsdevelopmentalchanges.He
alsoconductsastudyofthesetofallpossibletrajectoriesofthissystem.Withagreat
visionofthefundamentalchallengesposedbymorphogenesis,hewrites:
Theinterdependenceofthechemicalandmechanicaldata[describingthestateofagrowing
embryo]addsenormouslytothedifficulty,andattentionwillthereforebeconfined,sofar
asispossible,tocaseswherethesecanbeseparated.
Accordingly, Turing decides to focus his attention on simplified cases in which
mechanicalaspectscanbeignoredandchemicalaspectsarethemostsignificant.

412 A.Spicheretal.
Since Turing, it has become a widespread idea that dynamical systems offer
general principles for formalizing, understanding and designing self-organization
processes such as the ones encountered in morphogenesis. However, in the great
majority of models proposed to describe developmental processes, the shape
(mechanical data) and the content (chemical data) are clearly decoupled, making
thesemodelsusuallyfallintotwocategories2:
• formalismsthatfocusonpatternformationinaninitiallyhomogeneousbutstatic
substrate,and
• generative formalisms that specify the creation and the evolution of a dynamic
shape,irrespectiveoftheprocessesthatmaytakeplacewithintheshape.
Examples of the first category are given by reaction-diffusion systems, activator-
inhibitor models, or random Boolean networks. They rely on various model of
dynamicalsystems(Table16.1).ExamplesofthesecondcategoryincludeLinden-
mayersystems(L-systems)[36],membranecomputing[46],graphgrammars[49],
self-assemblysystems,andothers(Table16.2).

Inamultiset,allelementsareconsideredconnectedtoeachother.Inasequence,elementsareordered

involve a regular neighborhood, e.g., a rectangular lattice where each element has exactly four

16 Interaction-BasedModelingofMorphogenesisinMGS 413
DynamicalSystemswithaDynamicStructure(ds)2
Naturally,variousextensionsofexistingformalismshavebeenproposedtoaddress
the coupling of patterning and development, and blur the above distinction.
For example, the original L-systems have been later extended with the notion of
parameters [47]. This enabled models of plant growth and differentiation, such
as Anabaena Catenula, based on a chemical paradigm of reaction-diffusion by
activation-inhibition. On the one hand, a growth model specifies where diffusion
andreactionarepossiblewhile,ontheotherhand,concentrationsofchemicalscon-
trolgrowthrateandshapechange.
ThisextendedL-systemcanbeseenasadynamicalsystem,yetthesetofvariables

16.1.2 Space-Centric,Agent-CentricandInteraction-Oriented
Modeling
The seminal article of Turing introduces two models: a set of coupled ordinary
differentialequations(ODEs)andaformulationbasedonpartialdifferentialequa-
tions(PDEs).Intheformer,each cellischaracterized by theconcentration of two
morphogensandthecorrespondingequationsdescribetheexchangeofmorphogens
between two adjacent cells due to diffusion and the reactions within a cell. In the
latter, there are no cells: the system is described as a continuous medium where
morphogensdiffuseandreactineachpointofthedomain.Inbothcases,thespatial
structure (whether a set of cells or a continuous domain) is fixed a priori. If this
wherenotthecase,Turingwouldhavefacedthe“interdependence”problemthathe
himselfstressed,namelythecouplingbetweenthespatialstructureandtheprocesses
thattakeplaceinthisdomain.

ThesamedistinctionarisesincontinuummechanicsmodelsbetweentheEulerian

UnifyingtheAgent-CentricandtheSpace-CentricApproaches
Thisdistinctionhasasignificantimpactontheprogrammingstyleofthesimulation:
object-orientedlanguagesandmulti-agentsystemshavebeendevelopedtosupport
theagent-centricview,whilespatialcomputinglanguages[1, 3]supportthespace-
centric view. Leaving apart the difference of entities (“elementary piece of space”
vs. “agent”), the essential difference between the two approaches resides in the
expressionofthesystem’sevolution:

16 Interaction-BasedModelingofMorphogenesisinMGS 415
• intheagent-centricview,anentityevolvesbyreceivingamessagefromanother
entity,whereas
• inthespace-centricview,anentityevolvesbyqueryingitsneighborhood.
If the spatial structure is static, this neighborhood can be fixed a priori, but if it
is dynamic, then the difference between the two points of view starts to vanish.
“Elementary pieces of space” become agents that can be dynamically created and
rearrangedthroughtime.Thuswithrespecttothesimulationofmorphogenesis,the
two styles diverge only in the expression of the local evolution of an elementary
entity:triggeredbytheentityitselfortriggeredbyanotherentity.
Neitherframeworkissatisfactorybecausetheybothfocusonthelocalevolution
ofasingleentity,whichisnotexpressiveenough.Forexample,tohandletheproblem
ofcollisionofparticlesincellularautomata,onemusteitherconsideratwo-phase
evolution step (propagation and collision) [55] or turn to lattice gas automata, a
variantthatconsidersthecoupledevolutionofmorethanonecell[9].Inagent-based
modeling, the problem of coordinating synchronously several agents also leads to
thedevelopmentofmoreflexibleschemes[34, 38, 39].
We propose to overcome the limitation of both agent-based and space-based
approachesbyfocusingontheinteractionsbetweenentities(whetheragentsorele-
mentsofspace).Interactionsspecifythesimultaneousevolutionofa(usuallysmall)
subsetoftheentitiescomposingthesystem(Fig.16.2).

AShiftofPerspective
The interaction-based programming style reverses the perspective adopted in the
agent-based and spatial approaches.5 Instead of proceeding by first specifying the
elementsinthesystem,onemustdefinethetopologicalstructureA (i.e.,theneigh-
borhoodrelationships)allowingtheirinteractions.Thesubsystems(inparticular,the
componentsofthesystem)arethenidentifiedwiththecellsofA.
Inthisframework,thestateofa(ds)2simplycorrespondstotheassignationofa
(local)statetoeachcomponent.ThetopologyofA restrictsthepossibletransition
functionsofasubsystem S,asthecurrentstateof S onlydependsontheprevious
stateofSanditsneighbors.Now,however,Sisnotrestrictedtobeoneagentorone
spatialelement:itcanbeanyarbitrarypopulationofagentsoranarbitrarysubspace.
Furthermore,theevolution function notonlyspecifies theevolution oflocalstates
butalsotheircoupledevolutionwiththetopologyitself.
Suchastructureiswell-knowninalgebraictopology:thestateofa(ds)2 canbe
representedbyatopologicalchainthatassociatessomevalueswitheachcellofthe
cellularcomplex[33].
Overthepasthalf-century,therehavebeennotableeffortstodevelopcomprehen-
siveformulationsfromphysicsandgeometrybasedontopologicalchains.Theuse
ofchainsandcochainstostructurethemodelingandsimulationofphysicalsystems
canbetracedbacktoatleastthe1960swithBranin[6],whoappliedthesenotionsto
networkanalysisandcircuitdesign.Later,Tontiandco-authors[56, 57]developed
comprehensivediscreteformulationsofphysicallawsfromfirstprinciples[40, 58].
Several studies have subsequently developed this approach in the field of physical
modeling and computer-aided design (CAD), notably by Shapiro using the Chain
programming language [45] and various follow-up works [8, 12, 13, 15]. Chains
havealsobeenusedinnumericalcomputationasatooltostructureandgeneralize
thenotionof“mesh”[4].
One major goal of these studies is to unravel a proper set of definitions and
differentialoperatorsthatmakeitpossibletooperatethemachineryofmultivariate
calculusinafinitediscretespace.Themotivationistofindanequivalentcalculusthat
operatesintrinsicallyindiscretespace,withoutthereferencetothediscretizationof
anunderlyingcontinuousprocess.Thislineofresearchisparticularlydevelopedin
thefieldof“geometricmodeling”,withseveralrecentachievements[11, 31].
However, these works do not focus on the modeling of dynamical structures
in the way developed here. Their technical apparatus focuses on uniform compu-
tations and metric structures, whereas the MGS language (presented in the next

whohasdonearithmeticsimplifications:anarithmeticexpressioncanbesimplified

Atopologicalcollectioncanbeviewedasaslightgeneralizationofthenotionoffield.
Inphysics,afieldistheassignmentofaquantitytoeachpointofaspatialdomain[35].

MGS provides topological collections based on abstract cellular complexes. This
type of topological collections includes all the above types and extends to higher
dimensions. Yet, specific types (such as monoidal collections, GBFs, or proximal
collections) also have a usefulness because they correspond to specific topologies
thatcanbeimplementedmoreefficiently.
Finally,weintroduceawaytomakeadistinctionbetweenmultiplecollectionsof
thesametype,asitisoftenthecaseinnestedtopologicalcollections,usingsubtyping
(Sect.16.2.1.6).

Topologicalcollectionsareaweakenedversionofthenotionoftopologicalchain
developed in algebraic topology [43]. They were introduced in [24] to describe
arbitrarycomplexspatialstructuresthatappearinbiologicalsystems[25],andother
dynamical systems with a time varying structure [18, 29]. From the viewpoint of
computerscience,topologicalcollectionsarereminiscentofdata-fields,studiede.g.
by Lisper [32]. Data-fields are a generalization of the “array” data structure, in
which the set of indices is extended to finite subsets of Zn (see also [20]). With
topologicalcollections,incontrast,theunderlyingspaceisarbitrary.Infact,thetype
ofatopologicalcollectionisdeterminedbythechosenACC.
Graphs are a particularly important class of ACCs in MGS since it has been
showed in [23] how customary data structures (sets, lists, vectors, trees) can be
seenasgraph-basedtopologicalcollections:theelementsinadatastructurearethe
quantitiesassignedbythecollectiontothenodesofagraph;theincidencerelationship
correspondtotheedgesofthegraphandallowstheusualdatatraversal.
Inadditiontoscalarvalues(suchassymbols,Booleans,integers,floats,strings,
orlambda-expressions),thecurrentimplementationofMGSallowstheprogrammer
tohandleseveraltypesofcollections.Theelementsinacollectioncanbeanytype
ofvalues,includingcollections,thusachievingcomplexobjectsinthesenseof[7].
Throughexamples,weinformallysketchoutinthenextsectionsthecollectiontypes
thatweuse.

422 A.Spicheretal.
16.2.1.2 Records
An MGS record is a map that associates a value with a name called a slot. The
valuecanbeofanytype,includingotherrecordsorcollections.Accessingthevalue
of a slot from a record is achieved with a dot expression {a=1, b="red"}.b
evaluatestothestring"red".Newtypesofrecordscanbedefinedusingaspecific
syntax: for instance, record T = {a:int, b:string} defines a type T of
recordsinwhichslotsaandbarerespectivelylabeledbyanintegerandastring.
Thetopologiesassociatedwithrecordsarethe“totallydisconnected”ones:slots
in records have no neighbors. Seen as graphs, records’ vertices correspond to the
slotsandlabelstothevaluesassociatedwiththeslots—buttheyhavenoedges.
16.2.1.3 MonoidalCollections
Aspecificneighborhoodrelationshipthatplaysanimportantroleintherestofthis
chapter is the full relation. With this relation, every element in the collection is a
neighborofalltheotherelements.Thiscorrespondstothemultisetdatastructure.
Multisets,alongwithsetsandsequences,arecalledmonoidalcollectionsbecause
theycanbebuiltasmonoidsequippedwiththejoinoperator.Asequencecorresponds
to a join operation that has no special property except associativity; multisets are
obtainedwithanassociativeandcommutativejoin;andsetswhenthejoinoperator
is associative, commutative and idempotent. The join operator with its properties
inducesthetopologyofthecollectionanditsassociatedneighborhoodrelationship.
Itisalineargraphforsequencesandacompletegraphforsetsandmultisets.
We write a::m to add a value a in a monoidal collection m. The notations
seq:(), bag:() and set:() refer to the empty sequence, the empty multi-
setandtheemptyset,respectively.
16.2.1.4 Group-BasedFields
TopologicalcollectionscanbedefinedasGroup-BasedFields(GBF),whicharecon-
sideredasassociativearrayswhoseindicesareelementsofagroup[17].Thelatter
isdefinedbyafinitepresentation,i.e.,asetofgeneratorstogetherwithsomecon-
straintsontheircombination.ThusaGBFcanbepicturedasalabeledgraphwhere
theunderlyinggraphistheCayleygraphofthefinitepresentation.Thelabelsarethe
valuesassociatedwiththeverticesandthegeneratorsareassociatedwiththeedges.
Forinstance,inordertodefineaNEWSgrid(arectangularlatticeinwhicheach
nodehasfourneighbors)wemayusetwogeneratorse(east)andn(north),supporting
addition,differenceandmultiplicationbyaninteger,asillustratedinFig.16.6a.
Similarly, an hexagonal grid (6 neighbors for each vertex) can be defined by
meansofthreegeneratorsn,eandnw (north-west)andaconstraintn−nw = e,
asillustratedinFig.16.6b.Noticethatsuchgridisadequatetorepresentcellswitha
hexagonalshape,sincethegridcanbepavedwithhexagonscenteredonthepositions

the group). An equation v = w can be rewritten v−w = e and corresponds to a

16.2.2.2 Rules,TransformationsandApplicationStrategies
Atransformationisasetofrules.Whenatransformationisappliedtoacollection,
thedefaultstrategyistoapplythefirstruleasmanytimesaspossibleinparallel(a
rulecanbeappliedifitspatternmatchesasubcollection).Intheremainingcollec-
tion, the second rule is applied as many times as possible in parallel with the first
rule, and so on. This strategy is the maximal parallel application strategy used in
L-systems or in Paun systems [46]. Several other strategies are available in MGS
suchastheGillespieapplicationstrategybasedonGillespie’sstochasticsimulation
algorithm used to model chemical reactions [52]. Strategies provide a fine control
over the choice of the rules applied within a transformation. They are often non-
deterministic,i.e.,whenappliedtoacollectionC,onlyoneofthepossibleoutcomes
(randomlychosen)isreturnedbythetransformation.
A transformation T is a function like any other function and also a first-class
value.Itmeansthat,e.g.,atransformationcanbepassedasanargumenttoanother
functionorreturnedasaresult.Thisallowstocomposetransformationsveryeasily,
leadingtoahigher-orderfunctionalprogrammingstyle.
The expression T(c) denotes the application of one transformation step to the
collection c. A transformation step consists of applying the rules according to a
specificruleapplicationstrategy.Atransformationstepcanbeeffortlesslyiterated:

428 A.Spicheretal.
T[iter=n](c) denotestheapplicationofn transformationstepstoc,
T[fix](c) appliesthetransformationT untilafixedpointisreached.
Notethattransformationsarefirstclassvalues.
16.3 GrowthofaT-Shapein MGS
Inthissection,weshowMGSatworkbyelaboratingthreedifferentversionsofthe
same problem: the development of a T-shaped structure. This challenge has been
proposed as a benchmark example in [3] to compare different spatial computing
languages with each other, in particular how they behaved in three paradigmatic
tasks:(a)thecreationofacoordinatesystem,(b)thegrowthofastructureofagiven
shape, and (c) the patterning of this structure. Our interest here is to demonstrate
thebenefitsofaDSLapproachsuchastheMGSlanguage,i.e.,howtheflexibility
brought by the notions of “topological collection” and “transformation” makes it
possibletoaccommodate variousmodelingstylesandprogramreuse.Tothisaim,
wewilldevelopthreemodels:
• onebasedoncellularautomata’sregularspace-timelattice(Sect.16.3.1),
• one based on proximal collections, corresponding to an “amorphous medium”
underasynchronousevolution(Sect.16.3.2),
• oneusingcellularcomplexesin2D,startingfromanemptyspace(Sect.16.3.3).
In all models, we assume that the T-shapes start developing from one or a few
entitiesintwosuccessivephases:inthefirstgrowthphase(FGP),theprocessfollows
averticaldirectiontoformthestemoftheT.Inthesecondgrowthphase(SGP),the
twohorizontalsegmentsatthetopoftheTgrowinparallel.
Wewillnotelaboratehereonthepreliminaryacquisitionof“vertical”and“hor-
izontal” directions, as this type of positional information is not the focus of our
example.Therefore,inthecellularautomatamodel,wewilluseananisotropicneigh-
borhoodthatmakesthesedirectionsexplicit;intheamorphousmediummodel,we
willrelyonthecoordinatesofeachnode;andinthecellularcomplexmodel,somecell
wallswillreceivelabels(whichwillbeexplicitlypropagated)toguidetheasymmet-
ricgrowth.Obviously,acquiringpositionalinformationbylocalmeansinaninitial
symmetricmediumisaprobleminitself(seeforinstance[2, 14, 37, 44, 62]),but
theimplementationofthevariousalgorithmsproposedintheliteratureisrelatively
straightforward.AllexamplespresentedhereareactualMGSprograms(theMGS
interpreteranddocumentationareaccessibleathttp://mgs.spatial-computing.org).

16.3.3 CellularComplexImplementation

resentationof“empty”places“toinvade”.

EvolutionRules
We first specify the transformation used to compute the “mechanics” of the sys-
tem. For the sake of simplicity, we resort to mass-spring systems and Aristotelian
mechanics(i.e.,forceproportionaltospeed,notacceleration).Thisavoidstheburden
ofdoubleintegrationanddoesnotchangetheresults,becauseweareonlyinterested
inthefinalsteadystate.EveryedgeisaspringwithrestinglengthL0andcoefficient
k.Thetransformation
| let sum(acc, |                   | v)    | = { x=acc.x+v.x, |     | y=acc.y+v.y       | }             |
| ------------ | ----------------- | ----- | ---------------- | --- | ----------------- | ------------- |
| trans<2>     | MecaFace[k,L0,dt] |       |                  | = { |                   |               |
| f            | ⇒ let             | n =   | cardinal(icells( |     | (cid:4) f, 0)) in |               |
|              | let               | g =   | icellsfold(sum,  |     | {x=0.0,y=0.0},    | (cid:4) f, 0) |
|              | in                | f + { | x = g.x/n,       | y = | g.y/n }           |               |
}
onlymatchesthe2-cells(thisisspecifiedby<2>afterthetranskeyword).Para-
metersk,L0and dtareadditionalargumentsofthetransformation.Foreachcell
(cid:4)
f the number of 0-cells in its border is computed: f refers to the cell matched by
thepatternvariablef,andtheexpressionicells(f, (cid:4) 0)returnsthesetoffaces
(cid:4)
of f of dimension 0 (“i” stands for “incident”). Operator cardinal returns the

(cid:4)
the0-cellsintheborderoff.Thereductionfunctionisusedtocomputethecenter
ofmassofthecornersofparallelogramf.
Thenextsnippetofcodeintegratestheforcesandupdatesaccordinglytheposition
ofthevertices(Fig.16.12b):
| let | dist(v,             | v’)       | =              |     |      |            |     |
| --- | ------------------- | --------- | -------------- | --- | ---- | ---------- | --- |
|     | let d1              | = v’.x    | - v.x          | and | d2 = | v’.y - v.y |     |
|     | in sqrt(d1(cid:2)d1 |           | + d2(cid:2)d2) |     |      |            |     |
| let | sum1(v,             | acc,      | v’)            | =   |      |            |     |
|     | let d               | = dist(v, | v’)            | in  |      |            |     |
(cid:2)
|            | let f   | = k                         | (d -                                     | L0) / d             |                            |                       |              |
| ---------- | ------- | --------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | ------------------- | -------------------------- | --------------------- | ------------ |
|            |         | x=acc.x+f(cid:2)(v’.x-v.x), |                                          |                     | y=acc.y+f(cid:2)(v’.y-v.y) |                       |              |
|            | in {    |                             |                                          |                     |                            |                       | }            |
| let        | sum2(k, | v,                          | acc,                                     | f’) =               |                            |                       |              |
|            | let d   | = dist(f’,                  |                                          | v) in               |                            |                       |              |
|            |         |                             | (cid:2)                                  | sqrt(2.0)(cid:2)L0) |                            |                       |              |
|            | let f   | = k                         | (d -                                     |                     |                            | / d                   |              |
|            |         | x=acc.x+f(cid:2)(f’.x-v.x), |                                          |                     | y=acc.y+f(cid:2)(f’.y-v.y) |                       |              |
|            | in {    |                             |                                          |                     |                            |                       | }            |
| trans<0,1> |         | MecaVertex[k,L0,dt]         |                                          |                     | =                          | {                     |              |
| v          | ⇒ let   | Fspring=                    | neighborsfold(sum1(v),{x=0.0,y=0.0},v)in |                     |                            |                       |              |
|            | let     | Ftot                        | = icellsfold(sum2(k,                     |                     |                            | v),Fspring,           | (cid:4)v, 2) |

}
Thequalifier<0,1>meansthatwefocuson0-cellsandthattheneighborsfold
operationreliesonanotionofneighborhoodsuchthattwo0-cellsareneighborsiff
they are in the border of a common 1-cell (i.e., linked by an edge). The variable
Fspringcorrespondstothesummationofthespringforces,whileFtotiterates
onthecofacesofdimension2of(cid:4)v(therecanbeseveralsuchfaces,forinstanceat
thejunctionofthelinesformingtheT).Theideaisthateach2-cellexertsaninternal
pressurefromtheinsidetotheoutside,constrainingtheparallelogramtobeconvex.
The initial value of the fold is given by the forces computed with Fspring. The
“in”expression,simplyintegratesthetotalforcesoveratimestepdt.
The reduction functions sum1 and sum2 used in the folds are only partially
invoked: for example, the last two arguments of functions sum1 and sum2 are in
common (here: acc, v’) and provided by default by neighborsfold, so only
| sum1(v)andsum2(k, |     |     | v)arecalled. |     |     |     |     |
| ----------------- | --- | --- | ------------ | --- | --- | --- | --- |
Thefullmechanicalevolutionissimplygivenbycomputingoneevolutionstep
ofthefaceandoneevolutionstepofthevertices:
| fun | Meca(ch) |     | = MecaVertex(MecaFace(ch)) |     |     |     |     |
| --- | -------- | --- | -------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
Thereisoneadditionaltransformationtocomputethecelldivision:
| trans | Extrude   |         | = {             |        |          |     |     |
| ----- | --------- | ------- | --------------- | ------ | -------- | --- | --- |
|       | ∼v1       | < e12   | < ∼f:FGP        | > e12  | > ∼v2    |     |     |

|       | ⇒ letcell |         | v3 = new_vertex |        | ()       |     |     |
|       | and       | v4      | = new_vertex    |        | ()       |     |     |

|     | and       | f’ = new_acell(2,  |                | (e12,e23,e34,e41))              |               |
|     | in        | (v2 +              | { x=v2.x       | + (v2.x-f.x)(cid:2)random(0.1), |               |
|     |           |                    | y=v2.y         | + (v2.y-f.y)(cid:2)random(0.1)  | }) (cid:2) v3 |

Inthiscode,apattern∼vmatchesoneelementbutthiselementcanalsobematched
by another pattern (in the same transformation but not necessarily the same rule).
Contrarytotheclassicalpatternv,suchelementsarenotremovedfromtheresult.
Thefirstpattern
| ∼v1 | < e12 < | ∼f:FGP | > e12 | > ∼v2 / | (2=dim(f))&(e12=‘Apical) |
| --- | ------- | ------ | ----- | ------- | ------------------------ |
matches one face f, which must be FGP, one edge e12 labeled by the symbol
‘Apical,andthetwoverticesboundinge12.Theverticesandthefaceremainin
theresultbuttheedgeisremoved.Theright-handsidebuildsseveralnewcellsthat
replacee12(andupdatestheneighborhoodrelationshipsoftheremainingcells).
Whatisbuiltontheright-handsideisaparallelogram(newfacef’).Theedge
e12’replacesedgee12andisnowlabeledbythesymbol‘Internaltoprevent
furtherdevelopment.The“opposite”edgee23islabeledby‘Apicalinthefirst

|     |     | v3and | v4arethelabelsof |     | v2and v1,respectively,butupdated |

Thelabelsof
to have new positions. These positions are computed from the previous positions
shiftedbyasmallrandomnoise.Theparallelogramwillsoonunfoldbymechanical
evolutioninaccordancewiththeinternalpressureandthespringforce.
The complete evolution of the system is obtained by composing an extended
period of mechanical iteration (here, we arbitrarily chose 200 steps) with a single
growthstep:
| fun | Step(ch) | =   | Extrude(Meca[iter=200](ch)) |     |     |
| --- | -------- | --- | --------------------------- | --- | --- |
SnapshotsofthetrajectoryaregiveninFig.16.13.

17.1 Introduction
Engineershavemaderemarkableprogressintheirabilitytodesigncomplexproducts.
However,currentengineeringpracticestillfavorsatop-downapproach,wherethe
mainproblemismanuallydividedintosmalleronesinordertomaintaintheoverall
complexity reasonably manageable. This procedure is rather loosely defined and
ultimately relies on human expertise and creativity, which are skills that typically
involvehighcosts,areunreliable,andaredifficulttoformalize.Moreover,theever
increasingcomplexityofcurrentengineeredsystemsandrobustnessrequirementsis
reaching the feasibility limits of the current paradigm, forcing the implementation
ofnewengineeringmethodologies.
Inspired by the biological evolution and morphogenesis of organisms, recent
advances in the discipline of evolutionary computation propose a radically differ-
entapproach.Geneticalgorithmscombinedwithartificialdevelopmentmechanisms
operate over a population of individuals encoded by genomes that govern a mor-
phogeneticprocessproducingself-constructeddesigns[74].Thatis,thegenomeis
notablueprintofthedesign,butthesetofinstructionsthatindirectlybuildit.The
evolutionary operations (mutations and crossover) are applied to the developmen-
tal generative process that build the design, not to the design itself. This approach
hasbeenshowntoovercometheissuesofscalability,adaptability,andevolvability
present in traditional evolutionary algorithms (based on a genomic representation
thatencodesthedesigninaexplicitway)whenappliedtocomplexproblems[31].
Asaresult,evolutionarydevelopmentalalgorithmshavebeentriedinawiderange
of design problems, including the structure and controller of robots [23], digital
creaturesinArtificialLifestudies[4, 30, 35, 56, 73],andcomputeranimatedchar-
acters[71].Inalmostallmodels,however,thecontrolsystemisfairlycomplex(often
based on some kind of recurrent neural network), and in many cases, we believe,
unnecessarilyso.
Inaseminalwork[53],ChandanaPauldemonstratedthatawholebody-control
system is able to perform more complex computations than the control system
alone. This observation spawned the concept of morphological computation—a
design methodology for robotic-like agents to exploit the dynamics of interaction
betweenthebodyandthecontrolsystem,resultinginminimalcontrolsystems.Sev-
eral applications have been proposed in the field of robotics, including the design
ofsemi-passivebipedalrobots[48],tensegrityrobotswhosecomplex,couplednon-
lineardynamicsareharnessedtogenerateagaitpatternwithminimalcontrol[53],
androbotswithopen-loopcontrolsystemsandminimalnumbersofdegreesoffree-
dom that can self-stabilize into fast gait patterns and generate diverse behaviors
throughtheinteractionbetweenbodyandcontrolsystem[55].

In the following sections, we present three behavior-finding studies carried out
in our group: an evolutionary developmental model based on graph grammars
(Sect.17.2), a more refined version of the same model adding complex regulatory
mechanisms to the development (Sect.17.3), and a model solving a biologically
inspired behavior-finding problem, in which the agents do not undergo artificial
developmentperse,butaninnovativemutationoperatorprovidessomeoftheadvan-
tagesofartificialdevelopmentinthesenseofevolutionaryoptimization(Sect.17.4).

17.2.2 EvolutionarySearch
WetestedthepotentialofELSA3inanevolutionarysearchwiththefollowingprob-
lem:howavehicle,configuredasamass-springtensegritystructure,canlandprop-
erly when falling from a given height. In tensegrity structures, some edges bear

afterwards the free fall to the ground is physically simulated (with a given initial
velocityvector).Thefitnessofanindividualrepresentshow“well”itslandingwas,
bycalculatinghowfaritmovedfromatargetpointandhowtheimpactwasabsorbed.

genomesoftheindividualsformingthenewpopulationswereobtainedbymutating

•
Insertion:anewrandomgeneisinsertedatarandomposition.

Replacement:ageneisrandomlychosenandreplacedbyanewrandomgene.

randomattribute.
Theprobabilitiesofoccurrenceassociatedwitheachoperatorwere0.2,0.1,0.1and
0.6, respectively. A new generation was obtained by mutating and replacing 90%

whichtheprobabilityofselectionwaslinearlyproportionaltothefitness.Inorder
tominimizethedisruptionofmutations,theyweremorelikelytooccuratpositions
